original_hypothesis.title: LendingPool.decimals can revert for non-standard underlying tokens, cascading into share accounting failures
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LendingPool.decimals() calls IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals() and adds VIRTUAL_SHARE_DECIMALS. If underlyingToken does not implement decimals() or reverts, then any on-chain consumer querying pool decimals (including other contracts, DEXes, or collateral/oracle adapters) may revert. While not directly used in core arithmetic here, it can break integrations and potentially collateral valuation paths if decimals are queried on-chain elsewhere.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a practical integration hazard: some ERC20s are non-standard. If a pool is configured with such a token, the share token may become non-integrable and could block operations in other protocols that require decimals().
deployment_assumptions: A LendingPool is deployed with an underlyingToken that does not implement IERC20Metadata.decimals() correctly (reverts or returns unexpected values)
Critical protocol/integration path depends on decimals() call success
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LendingPool.initialize sets underlyingToken without validation; decimals() assumes IERC20Metadata compliance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals() external call without try/catch or validation

preconditions: Underlying token decimals() call reverts or is non-standard

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert can break integrations; incorrect decimals can confuse accounting in external systems
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation during initialize; no fallback decimals

analysis_questions: Does protocol restrict underlying tokens to a vetted list with standard decimals behavior?
Are any on-chain components relying on decimals() for collateral valuation or share math?
What happens if decimals() returns extremely large values (overflow in external consumers)?
validation_requirements: Review deployment configuration for underlyingToken whitelist/standards
Test with a mock ERC20 without decimals() to observe behavior in integrations
Confirm core accounting does not rely on decimals() anywhere else

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If misconfigured with a non-standard token, integrations or wrappers interacting with the pool's share token revert on decimals(), causing operational DoS and potentially trapping user funds in external protocols.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or addition of non-standard underlying token

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potentially material via integration breakage/trapped funds in third-party protocols
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users interacting through integrations that depend on decimals() success
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Redeploy pool or upgrade with defensive decimals handling

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-IERC20Metadata underlying tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Watch for decimals() call reverts in indexing/integrations

validation_result.mechanism: missing input validation on underlying token IERC20Metadata compliance
validation_result.impact_summary: LendingPool.decimals() makes an unguarded external call to IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals(). If a pool is initialized with a non-standard token lacking decimals(), the pool's share token becomes non-ERC20-compliant, breaking integrations.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a defensive programming gap in LendingPool.sol. The decimals() function (line 61-63) overrides the ERC20 decimals() to return `IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals() + VIRTUAL_SHARE_DECIMALS`. This external call has no try/catch, no fallback default, and no validation during initialize(). If the underlying token does not implement decimals() or reverts, the entire LendingPool share token becomes non-compliant with ERC20Metadata, which breaks any on-chain or off-chain consumer querying decimals(). Notably, Api3OracleReader.getPrice_e36() (line 71) also calls IERC20Metadata(_token).decimals() without protection, so the token needs decimals() for the oracle to function. However, the core share accounting math (_toShares, _toAmt) does NOT depend on decimals(), so deposits/withdrawals/borrows/repays would still function correctly for tokens that revert on decimals(). The defect is the missing validation at initialization time and the missing defensive handling in the view function.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: LendingPool.sol lines 61-63 confirm the exact pattern claimed. The initialize() function at lines 47-58 sets underlyingToken without any validation of IERC20Metadata compliance. The code casts directly to IERC20Metadata and calls decimals() with no try/catch or fallback.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: External call to underlyingToken.decimals() with no try/catch or fallback. If underlyingToken doesn't implement IERC20Metadata.decimals(), this reverts, making the LendingPool share token non-ERC20-compliant.
No validation that _underlyingToken implements IERC20Metadata.decimals(). A try/catch call to decimals() during initialization would catch non-compliant tokens early.

calling_sequence: 1. Any caller (on-chain contract or off-chain) calls LendingPool.decimals()
3. If underlyingToken doesn't implement decimals() or reverts, the call reverts
4. The LendingPool share token violates ERC20Metadata interface
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: LendingPool.decimals() at line 62 makes an external call `IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals()`. The underlyingToken is set at initialization (line 48) without any validation. If the token doesn't implement decimals(), the external call reverts with no fallback. This also affects Api3OracleReader.getPrice_e36() at line 71 which similarly calls `IERC20Metadata(_token).decimals()` without protection.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A LendingPool is deployed with an underlyingToken that does not implement IERC20Metadata.decimals() correctly
Critical protocol/integration path depends on decimals() call success
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that specify which tokens are used as underlyingToken in LendingPool deployments. Only foundry.toml and remappings.txt are available, which don't specify deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario per se but a misconfiguration hazard. If a LendingPool is deployed with a non-standard token (e.g., one that doesn't implement decimals() like some older ERC20 tokens), then: (1) LendingPool.decimals() reverts for any caller, (2) the share token is non-ERC20-compliant, (3) DEXes, wallets, and other protocols that query decimals() will fail, (4) Api3OracleReader.getPrice_e36() would also revert for that token. Core accounting (mint, burn, borrow, repay) does NOT depend on decimals() and would still function.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. The core protocol accounting is unaffected since _toShares/_toAmt don't use decimals(). However, the LendingPool share token becomes non-ERC20-compliant, breaking: (1) third-party integrations querying decimals(), (2) oracle price calculations in Api3OracleReader, (3) any composability with DEXes or other DeFi protocols. The practical likelihood is reduced because the protocol's own oracle also requires decimals(), creating a natural constraint on supported tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Add a try/catch around IERC20Metadata(underlyingToken).decimals() in the decimals() function with a sensible fallback default (e.g., 18)
Add validation in initialize() that calls underlyingToken.decimals() and reverts early if the token doesn't support it
Document supported token requirements explicitly in the initialize function's NatSpec
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC20 without decimals() function. 2. Deploy LendingPool with this token as underlyingToken. 3. Call LendingPool.decimals() and observe revert. 4. Verify core operations (mint, burn) still work despite decimals() reverting. 5. Verify Api3OracleReader.getPrice_e36() also reverts for this token.
uncertainties: Without deployment scripts, it's unknown which tokens will actually be used as underlying tokens
The protocol may have off-chain governance processes that vet tokens before deployment
The practical likelihood depends on whether non-standard tokens would ever be considered
additional_testing_needed: Test with tokens that return non-uint8 from decimals() (e.g., uint256)
Test with tokens where decimals() returns very large values (potential overflow when adding VIRTUAL_SHARE_DECIMALS = 8)
Verify that tokens returning decimals > 247 would cause uint8 overflow in decimals() + 8
